perturbation.original_text: 2.2 The seller shall be confirmed within three working days after receipt of order. If the seller finds order is not acceptable or need to modify, should note entrusted party in two working days after receipt of the order, If the seller did not confirm orders in time or notice not accept orders or modifications, the seller is deemed to have been accepted the order. The orders become effective once the seller accepts, any party shall not unilaterally cancel the order before the two sides agreed .
perturbation.changed_text: 2.2 The seller shall endeavor to confirm within three working days after receipt of order. If the seller finds order is not acceptable or need to modify, should note entrusted party within a reasonable timeframe after receipt of the order. The orders become effective once the seller accepts, any party may unilaterally cancel the order with a written notice of 30 days to the other party.
perturbation.explanation: The change in Section 2.2 introduces several contradictions. First, 'shall be confirmed' is weakened to 'shall endeavor to confirm,' making the confirmation process discretionary. Second, the deadline for notifying modifications changes from 'two working days' to 'a reasonable timeframe'. Lastly, it allows for unilateral cancellation of the order with a written notice, which directly contradicts the original statement of 'any party shall not unilaterally cancel the order before the two sides agreed'. This creates a contradiction if other sections expect orders to be binding unless mutually agreed upon, or rely on timelines for confirmation/modification response.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.2 The seller shall endeavor to confirm within three working days after receipt of order. If the seller finds order is not acceptable or need to modify, should note entrusted party within a reasonable timeframe after receipt of the order. The orders become effective once the seller accepts, any party may unilaterally cancel the order with a written notice of 30 days to the other party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text allows for unilateral cancellation of the order with 30 days' notice, directly contradicting the original intent that orders are binding once accepted and cannot be unilaterally cancelled without mutual agreement. This change introduces a strong contradiction because it fundamentally alters the conditions under which an order can be terminated, creating uncertainty and potential disputes over the enforceability of the contract. The contradicted text is the changed text itself.

perturbation.original_text: 15. GUARANTEE OF QUALITY: The Sellers guarantee that the commodity hereof is complies in all respects with the quality and specification stipulated in this Contract.
perturbation.changed_text: 15. GUARANTEE OF QUALITY: The Sellers will make commercially reasonable efforts to ensure that the commodity hereof complies in most respects with the quality and specification stipulated in this Contract.
perturbation.explanation: Section 15 originally stated that 'The Sellers guarantee that the commodity hereof complies in all respects with the quality and specification stipulated in this Contract.' The changed text alters this to 'The Sellers will make commercially reasonable efforts to ensure that the commodity hereof complies in most respects with the quality and specification stipulated in this Contract.' This change replaces a firm guarantee with a commitment to 'commercially reasonable efforts,' and changes 'in all respects' to 'in most respects'. This directly contradicts Section 16 (CLAIMS), where the original strict compliance is the baseline for claims. The change reduces seller liability for non-compliance and opens the door for disputes over what constitutes 'commercially reasonable efforts' and 'most respects,' impacting the enforceability of quality guarantees.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 16. CLAIMS: Within 7 days after the arrival of the goods at destination, should the quality, specification, or quantity be found not in conformity with the stipulations of the Contract except those claims for which the insurance company or the owners of the vessel are liable, the Buyers, on the strength of the Inspection Certificate issued by the China Commodity Inspection Bureau, have the right to claim for replacement with new goods, or for compensation, and all the expenses (such as inspection charges, freight for returning the goods and for sending the replacement, insurance premium, storage and loading and unloading charges etc.) shall be borne by the Sellers. The Certificate so issued shall be accepted as the base of a claim. The Sellers, in accordance with the Buyers' claim, shall be responsible for the immediate elimination of the defect(s), complete or partial replacement of the commodity or shall devaluate the commodity according to the state of defect(s). Where necessary, the Buyers shall be at liberty to eliminate the defect(s) themselves at the Sellers' expenses. If the Sellers fail to answer the Buyers within one weeks after receipt of the aforesaid claim, the claim shall be reckoned as having been accepted by the Sellers.
perturbation.justification: The original text in Section 15 guaranteed that the commodity would comply 'in all respects' with the stipulated quality and specification. Section 16, 'CLAIMS,' then allows buyers to claim for replacement or compensation if the quality or specification is 'not in conformity with the stipulations of the Contract.' The changed text in Section 15 weakens the guarantee to 'commercially reasonable efforts' to ensure compliance 'in most respects.' This directly contradicts Section 16, because the baseline for a claim (strict conformity with the contract) is undermined. The change makes it unclear whether a claim is valid if the commodity complies in 'most respects' but not 'all respects,' thus contradicting the claims process stipulated in Section 16. The change alters the Sellers' liability. Therefor there exists a strong contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 18. LATE DELIVERY AND PENALTY: Should the Sellers fail to make delivery on time as stipulated in the Contract, with exception of Force Majeure causes specified in Clause 17 of this Contract, the Buyers shall agree to postpone the delivery on condition that the Sellers agree to pay a penalty which shall be deducted by the paying bank from the payment. The penalty, however, shall not exceed 5% of the total value of the goods involved in the late delivery. The rate of penalty is charged at 0.5% for every seven days, odd days less than seven days should be counted as seven days. In case the Sellers fail to make delivery ten weeks later than the time of shipment stipulated in the Contract, the Buyers have the right to cancel the contract and the Sellers, in spite of the cancellation, shall still pay the aforesaid penalty to the Buyers without delay, the seller should refund the money received and pay the 30% of the total goods price of the penalty
perturbation.changed_text: 18. LATE DELIVERY AND PENALTY: Should the Sellers fail to make delivery on time as stipulated in the Contract, with exception of Force Majeure causes specified in Clause 17 of this Contract, the Buyers may, at their sole discretion, consider postponing the delivery, and any penalty for late delivery will be subject to negotiation between the parties. The penalty, however, may be waived entirely by the Buyer. In case the Sellers fail to make delivery ten weeks later than the time of shipment stipulated in the Contract, the Buyers may cancel the contract, but the penalty payment for the late delivery is not guaranteed to be paid to the Buyers.
perturbation.explanation: Section 18 defined clear penalties for late delivery. The modified text replaces the 'shall agree to postpone' with 'may, at their sole discretion, consider postponing the delivery', and replaced 'the Sellers agree to pay a penalty which shall be deducted by the paying bank from the payment' to 'any penalty for late delivery will be subject to negotiation between the parties'. Furthermore, it is mentioned 'the penalty, however, may be waived entirely by the Buyer.' It also weakens the cancellation clause by stating that the penalty payment is 'not guaranteed to be paid to the Buyers'. This contrasts with the original requirement for penalty payments even if the contract is canceled. This in-text contradiction creates uncertainty on the penalties, as the initial contract had set in stone rules on late delivery. This could affect the interpretation and enforcement of the late delivery clause.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 18. LATE DELIVERY AND PENALTY: Should the Sellers fail to make delivery on time as stipulated in the Contract, with exception of Force Majeure causes specified in Clause 17 of this Contract, the Buyers may, at their sole discretion, consider postponing the delivery, and any penalty for late delivery will be subject to negotiation between the parties. The penalty, however, may be waived entirely by the Buyer. In case the Sellers fail to make delivery ten weeks later than the time of shipment stipulated in the Contract, the Buyers may cancel the contract, but the penalty payment for the late delivery is not guaranteed to be paid to the Buyers.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies mandatory penalties for late delivery, while the changed text makes penalties negotiable and waivable. This directly contradicts the original intent and creates uncertainty, therefore the contradiction exists. The contradicted text and changed text are actually the same in this case, however, the provided 'Perturbation Explanation' clearly states the impact that the changed text had in creating the contradiction in-text.
